Title: The biosynthetic pathway of potato solanidanes diverged from that of spirosolanes due to evolution of a dioxygenase

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High in tuber sprouts; similar expression pattern to previously identified SGA biosynthetic genes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in tuber sprouts; analyzed in leaves, stems, flowers, roots, stolons, tuber peels, and tuber sprouts
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding with α-solamarine, β-solamarine, spirosol aglycone, or α-tomatine; inhibition by prohexadione (DOX inhibitor) but not uniconazole-P; in vitro assays require 2-oxoglutarate, Fe2+, ascorbate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi silencing in potato; feeding assays in St16DOX-disrupted potato hairy roots; recombinant protein enzyme assay in E. coli; LC-MS metabolite profiling; HRMS/NMR structural identification; 18O2 labeling; kinetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for reducing toxic potato glycoalkaloids α-solanine and α-chaconine in breeding or genome editing; could be downregulated/edited to lower antinutritional solanidane SGAs, but may cause accumulation of spirosolane SGAs and potentially alter defense
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion of spirosolane-type steroidal glycoalkaloids to solanidane-type intermediates via C-16α hydroxylation-triggered ring rearrangement, generating an iminium/zwitterionic solanidane intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing greatly reduces α-solanine and α-chaconine and causes accumulation of α-solamarine and β-solamarine; possible effects on plant chemical defense are implied
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves; tuber sprouts are major expression site relevant to SGA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms a committed intermediate toward solanidane glycoalkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DPS prefers glycosylated spirosolanes over aglycones by ~1123-fold in catalytic efficiency, indicating conversion occurs after C-3 glycosylation; DPS product is an intermediate and additional reductase step(s) are required in planta to complete solanidane formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPS was identified as the key potato enzyme required for conversion of spirosolane SGAs to solanidane SGAs. 2. RNAi silencing of DPS reduced α-solanine and α-chaconine by ~90% and caused accumulation of α-solamarine and β-solamarine. 3. Recombinant DPS converted α-solamarine, β-solamarine, α-tomatine, and corresponding aglycones into solanidane-type intermediates via an unusual ring-rearrangement reaction. 4. DPS strongly prefers glycosylated substrates, indicating branch conversion occurs after glycosylation.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S constitutive promoter for potato RNAi and tomato overexpression constructs
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Steroidal glycoalkaloids function in defense against pathogens and herbivores; α-solanine and α-chaconine are toxic and bitter antinutritional compounds affecting food quality
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes biosynthesis of toxic solanidane glycoalkaloids α-solanine and α-chaconine; loss of function shifts accumulation toward spirosolane glycoalkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq expression data from Spud DB; FPKM-based candidate selection
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Solanidane-type iminium/zwitterionic intermediate from α-solamarine, β-solamarine, α-tomatine, and related spirosolanes
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branch conversion from spirosolane to solanidane aglycone/glycoalkaloid scaffold

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in potato tuber sprouts; not silenced in DPSi lines
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro enzyme assays with spirosolane substrates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein enzyme assay; kinetic analysis; qRT-PCR off-target check in DPS RNAi plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Minor auxiliary target when engineering potato SGA profile; likely less influential than DPS
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the same spirosolane-to-solanidane intermediate conversion as DPS in vitro
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential minor contribution to solanidane formation; lower substrate preference for glycosides than DPS
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms intermediate toward solanidane glycoalkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shares 85.8% amino acid identity with DPS and has a longer C-terminus; likely a minor contributor in planta because DPS silencing caused strong phenotype without off-target suppression of St7070
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. St7070 is a close DPS homolog in the potato chromosome 1 DOX cluster. 2. Recombinant St7070 catalyzed the same ring-rearrangement reaction as DPS. 3. St7070 had much weaker preference for glycosylated substrates than DPS, suggesting distinct physiological contribution. 4. It may contribute only marginally to potato solanidane glycoalkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Steroidal glycoalkaloids contribute to defense but are antinutritional in food crops
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely minor positive contributor to solanidane glycoalkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq expression data from Spud DB
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branch conversion from spirosolane to solanidane scaffold
